event: Neolithic farming communities spread across the Italian peninsula.
Bronze Age cultures develop across northern and central Italy.
The Villanovan culture, ancestral to the Etruscans, emerges in central Italy.
The Etruscan civilization rises, building sophisticated cities and a distinctive culture.
Rome is traditionally founded, by legend on the Palatine Hill by Romulus.
The Roman Republic is established after the overthrow of the last Etruscan king.
The First Punic War begins Rome's long struggle with Carthage for Mediterranean dominance.
Hannibal crosses the Alps to invade Italy during the Second Punic War.
Rome destroys Carthage, securing supremacy in the western Mediterranean.
Julius Caesar is assassinated on the Ides of March, plunging Rome into civil war.
Augustus becomes the first Roman emperor, inaugurating the Empire and the Pax Romana.
The eruption of Mount Vesuvius buries Pompeii and Herculaneum.
The Roman Empire reaches its greatest territorial extent under Trajan.
Emperor Constantine's Edict of Milan grants religious tolerance to Christians.
The Roman Empire permanently divides into eastern and western halves.
Visigoths under Alaric sack Rome, signaling the decline of Western power.
Odoacer deposes Romulus Augustulus, ending the Western Roman Empire.
The Lombards invade Italy and establish a kingdom across much of the peninsula.
Charlemagne conquers the Lombard kingdom and absorbs northern Italy into the Frankish realm.
Charlemagne is crowned Emperor in Rome by Pope Leo III.
Otto I is crowned emperor, asserting Holy Roman authority over northern Italy.
Roger II founds the Norman Kingdom of Sicily, uniting southern Italy under one crown.
Marco Polo departs Venice on his celebrated journey to Asia.
Maritime republics like Venice and Genoa dominate Mediterranean trade and banking.
The Black Death sweeps through Italy, killing a large share of the population.
The Italian Renaissance flourishes in Florence, transforming art, science, and thought.
The French invasion begins the decades-long Italian Wars among European powers.
The Peace of Cateau-Cambresis ends the Italian Wars, confirming Spanish dominance.
Napoleon Bonaparte invades Italy, reshaping the peninsula and spreading revolutionary ideals.
The Congress of Vienna restores conservative monarchies and Austrian influence over a fragmented Italy.
Revolutions and the First Italian War of Independence erupt amid rising nationalism.
The Kingdom of Italy is proclaimed under Victor Emmanuel II, unifying most of the peninsula.
Italian troops capture Rome, completing unification and making the city the capital.
Italy enters World War I on the side of the Allies.
Mussolini's March on Rome brings the Fascists to power, beginning a dictatorship.
The Lateran Treaty resolves the Roman Question and establishes Vatican City as an independent state.
Italy enters World War II as an Axis power alongside Nazi Germany.
Mussolini is deposed and Italy signs an armistice with the Allies, who advance up the peninsula.
Italians vote in a referendum to abolish the monarchy and establish a republic.
Italy becomes a founding member of NATO.
Italy is admitted to the United Nations.
Italy signs the Treaty of Rome, becoming a founding member of the European Economic Community.
Rome hosts the Summer Olympic Games, symbolizing the postwar economic miracle.
Former Prime Minister Aldo Moro is kidnapped and murdered by the Red Brigades during the Years of Lead.
The Mani Pulite corruption investigations expose widespread graft, collapsing the postwar party system.
Italy adopts the euro, with notes and coins replacing the lira.
A powerful earthquake devastates the city of L'Aquila in the Abruzzo region.
Italy becomes Europe's early epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic, imposing a nationwide lockdown.
Italy wins the UEFA European Football Championship, defeating England in the final at Wembley.
